---
title: "Wildfire and Smoke"
format: html
---

# 🔥 Wildfire & Smoke: Creating a Defensible Sanctuary

[cite_start]Goal: To prevent building ignition from wind-blown embers and maintain "Clean Air" zones to protect vulnerable residents from hazardous smoke. [cite: 1]

-----

## 1. Foundational Resilience (One-Time Upgrades)

[cite_start]These "once-and-done" structural improvements significantly increase the chance of a building surviving a wildfire without active intervention. [cite: 1]

  - **Fire Department Audit:** Invite your local fire department to conduct a professional wildfire risk audit. [cite_start]They can provide specific countermeasures tailored to your local terrain. [cite: 1]
  - **Roofing & Eaves:** Evaluate roofing material to ensure it is non-combustible (e.g., metal, tile, or Class A asphalt). [cite_start]Ensure all eaves are "boxed" or closed to prevent embers from being trapped. [cite: 1]
  - **Siding & Vents:** Replace combustible siding with fire-resistant materials (fiber cement, brick, stone). [cite_start]Cover all external vents with fine wire mesh. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Propane Safety:** Ensure any propane tanks are located at least 15 feet away from any building structure. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Evacuation Planning:** Develop a formal evacuation plan that includes primary and secondary routes for the congregation and any weekday programs (like daycares). [cite: 1]

-----

## 2. The Defensible Space System

[cite_start]Wildfire defense is about managing "fuel" in concentric circles around the church. [cite: 1]

### The 0–5 Foot "No-Burn Zone"

  - [cite_start]**Remove Combustibles:** Remove all mulch, woody shrubs, and debris within 5 feet of the church walls. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Hardscaping:** Replace vegetation in this zone with gravel, stone, or concrete to prevent fire from touching the building. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Roof & Gutters:** Ensure roof valleys and gutters are cleared of pine needles and leaves, which are the primary ignition points for embers. [cite: 1]

### The 5–30 Foot "Lean & Clean Zone"

  - [cite_start]**Minimize Vegetation:** Keep this area well-irrigated and free of dead wood. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Tree Grooming:** Trim trees so that no branches are lower than 15 feet from the ground to prevent "ladder fires" from reaching the canopy. [cite: 1]

### The 30–100 Foot "Reduced Fuel Zone"

  - [cite_start]**Thinning:** Remove the most flammable vegetation (such as junipers or dry brush) and create "breaks" between clusters of trees to slow a fire's spread. [cite: 1]

-----

## 3. Smoke Resilience & Clean Air Zones

[cite_start]During regional fires, the church can serve as a "Clean Air Center" for those with respiratory issues or those without air conditioning. [cite: 1]

  - **HVAC Filtration:** Upgrade your system to handle MERV 13 or HEPA filters. [cite_start]These are high-density filters capable of scrubbing fine smoke particles (PM2.5) from the air. [cite: 1]
  - **Portable Air Scrubbers:** Purchase industrial-grade portable air purifiers. [cite_start]These can be deployed in the "Fellowship Hall" or Sanctuary to create a safe breathing environment even if the main HVAC is limited. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**DIY Air Scrubbing:** There are also plans for DIY air purifiers made from a box fan, MERV 13 filters, and duct tape that are surprisingly effective. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Monitoring:** Keep a digital AQI (Air Quality Index) monitor on-site to verify that the indoor air remains safe when outdoor conditions are hazardous. [cite: 1]

-----

## 4. Annual Maintenance & Readiness

Consistency is key to fire safety. [cite_start]Add these tasks to your church's seasonal calendar. [cite: 1]

  - [cite_start]**Vegetation Management:** Trim back spring growth and clear brush near buildings every year before the dry season. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Gutter Maintenance:** Clean gutters frequently or install fire-rated gutter covers to prevent debris accumulation. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Evacuation Drills:** Conduct an annual drill to ensure staff and volunteers know how to clear the building quickly and safely. [cite: 1]
  - [cite_start]**Fire Department Audit:** Invite your local fire department to conduct a professional wildfire risk audit. [cite: 1]
